title: Max-Cut on a 6-Node Graph
summary: Partition a graph's nodes into two groups to maximize the number of edges crossing between them.
problem_statement: I have a graph with 6 nodes labeled 0 through 5 and edges: (0,1), (0,2), (1,3), (2,3), (2,4), (3,5), (4,5). Partition the nodes into two groups so that the number of edges crossing between the groups is maximized. Tell me which nodes go in each group.
real_world_example: Designing a two-team tournament so as many rivalries as possible are between teams (not within). Or partitioning a circuit into two chips to minimize cross-chip wires (the dual problem).

expected_pattern.variables: s_i in {-1, +1} — which group node i belongs to
expected_pattern.objective: maximize sum over edges (i,j) of (1 - s_i * s_j) / 2

expected_solution_summary: The graph is bipartite — Group A = {0, 3, 4}, Group B = {1, 2, 5} cuts all 7 edges.
learning_notes: Max-Cut is the textbook example of a quadratic-objective, constraint-free QUBO. It maps directly into Ising form. The 'group A vs group B' symmetry means there are always at least two equivalent optima — the solver may give you either.

learning_objectives: See an Ising / QUBO that has zero constraints — the structure is in the quadratic objective
Map a graph problem onto pairwise spin couplings
Notice that symmetric problems have symmetric optimal sets (≥2 equivalent answers)
